content:
# Numbers Chapter 22 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 174-176 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0129-0130 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_022_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_003.jpg`; `NUM_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### Balak Sends for Balaam (vv. 1-6)

**1** And the Sons of Israel set out and encamped in the plains of Moab, beyond the Jordan at Jericho.

**2** And Balak the son of Zippor saw all that Israel had done to the Amorites.

**3** And Moab was exceedingly afraid because of the people, for they were many. And Moab was filled with dread because of the Sons of Israel.

**4** And Moab said to the elders of Midian, "Now this assembly will lick up all that is around us, as the ox licks up the green of the field." And Balak the son of Zippor was king of Moab at that time.

**5** And he sent messengers to Balaam the son of Beor, to Pethor, which is by the River, to the land of the sons of his people, to call him, saying, "Behold, a people has come out from Egypt. Behold, they cover the eye of the land, and they are dwelling opposite me.

**6** And now, come, please, curse this people for me, for they are mightier than I. Perhaps I shall be able to strike them, and I shall drive them out from the land. For I know that he whom you bless is surely blessed, and he whom you curse is surely cursed."

---

### God Forbids Balaam to Go (vv. 7-14)

**7** And the elders of Moab and the elders of Midian went with the fees for divination in their hand. And they came to Balaam and spoke to him the words of Balak.

**8** And he said to them, "Lodge here tonight, and I will bring back word to you, as YHWH shall speak to me." And the princes of Moab stayed with Balaam.

**9** And God came to Balaam and said, "Who are these men with you?"

**10** And Balaam said to God, "Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, has sent to me, saying,

**11** 'Behold, the people that has come out from Egypt, it covers the eye of the land. Now come, curse them for me. Perhaps I shall be able to fight against them and drive them out.'"

**12** And God said to Balaam, "You shall not go with them. You shall not curse the people, for they are blessed."

**13** And Balaam rose in the morning and said to the princes of Balak, "Go to your land, for YHWH has refused to let me go with you."

**14** And the princes of Moab rose and went to Balak and said, "Balaam refused to come with us."

---

### Balak Sends Again (vv. 15-21)

**15** And Balak again sent princes, more numerous and more honored than these.

**16** And they came to Balaam and said to him, "Thus says Balak the son of Zippor: 'Let nothing, I pray you, hinder you from coming to me,

**17** for I will surely honor you greatly, and all that you say to me I will do. And come, please, curse this people for me.'"

**18** And Balaam answered and said to the servants of Balak, "If Balak were to give me his house full of silver and gold, I could not go beyond the mouth of YHWH my God, to do a small thing or a great thing.

**19** And now, stay here, you also, tonight, that I may know what more YHWH will speak with me."

**20** And God came to Balaam at night and said to him, "If the men have come to call you, rise, go with them. But only the word that I speak to you, that you shall do."

**21** And Balaam rose in the morning and saddled his donkey and went with the princes of Moab.

---

### The Angel and the Donkey (vv. 22-35)

**22** And the anger of God was kindled because he was going, and the angel of YHWH stationed himself in the way as an adversary against him. And he was riding upon his donkey, and his two servants were with him.

**23** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH standing in the way, with his drawn sword in his hand. And the donkey turned aside out of the way and went into the field. And Balaam struck the donkey to turn her back onto the way.

**24** And the angel of YHWH stood in a narrow path of the vineyards, a wall on this side and a wall on that side.

**25** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH, and she pressed herself against the wall and pressed the foot of Balaam against the wall. And he struck her again.

**26** And the angel of YHWH passed on further and stood in a narrow place where there was no way to turn either to the right or to the left.

**27** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH, and she lay down under Balaam. And the anger of Balaam was kindled, and he struck the donkey with the staff.

**28** And YHWH opened the mouth of the donkey, and she said to Balaam, "What have I done to you, that you have struck me these three times?"

**29** And Balaam said to the donkey, "Because you have mocked me. If only there were a sword in my hand, for now I would have killed you."

**30** And the donkey said to Balaam, "Am I not your donkey, upon which you have ridden all your life long to this day? Have I been accustomed to doing thus to you?" And he said, "No."

**31** And YHWH uncovered the eyes of Balaam, and he saw the angel of YHWH standing in the way, with his drawn sword in his hand. And he bowed his head and fell on his face.

**32** And the angel of YHWH said to him, "Why have you struck your donkey these three times? Behold, I went out as an adversary, for your way is reckless before me.

**33** And the donkey saw me and turned aside before me these three times. If she had not turned aside from before me, surely now I would have killed you and let her live."

**34** And Balaam said to the angel of YHWH, "I have sinned, for I did not know that you were standing in the way to oppose me. And now, if it is evil in your eyes, I will return."

**35** And the angel of YHWH said to Balaam, "Go with the men. But only the word that I speak to you, that you shall speak." And Balaam went with the princes of Balak.

---

### Balaam Arrives in Moab (vv. 36-41)

**36** And Balak heard that Balaam had come, and he went out to meet him at the city of Moab, which is on the border of the Arnon, which is at the extremity of the border.

**37** And Balak said to Balaam, "Did I not surely send to you to call you? Why did you not come to me? Am I not indeed able to honor you?"

**38** And Balaam said to Balak, "Behold, I have come to you now. Am I at all able to speak anything? The word that God puts in my mouth, that I shall speak."

**39** And Balaam went with Balak, and they came to Kiriath-huzoth.

**40** And Balak sacrificed cattle and sheep, and he sent portions to Balaam and to the princes who were with him.

**41** And it came to pass in the morning that Balak took Balaam and brought him up to Bamoth-baal, and he saw from there the edge of the people.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **v. 1 -- "the plains of Moab" (בְּעַרְבוֹת מוֹאָב):** The Leningrad Codex reads בְּעַרְבוֹת מוֹאָב. The noun עֲרָבָה denotes a dry, arid plain; the plural construct form indicates the broad flatlands east of the Jordan opposite Jericho. This becomes the staging ground for the rest of Numbers and Deuteronomy.

2. **v. 1 -- "beyond the Jordan at Jericho" (מֵעֵבֶר לְיַרְדֵּן יְרֵחוֹ):** The phrase מֵעֵבֶר indicates the eastern side from the narrator's Cisjordan perspective. The Leningrad Codex has יְרֵחוֹ without a preposition, functioning as a locative of reference ("the Jordan of Jericho" or "the Jordan at Jericho").

3. **v. 3 -- "was filled with dread" (וַיָּקׇץ):** The verb קוץ means "to feel a loathing dread" or "to be in dread." The LXX (Vaticanus) renders προσώχθισεν ("was vexed/disgusted"), preserving the sense of visceral revulsion. The doubling of Moab's fear (יָגָר in v. 3a, וַיָּקׇץ in v. 3b) is a rhetorical intensification.

4. **v. 4 -- "as the ox licks up" (כִּלְחֹךְ הַשּׁוֹר):** A vivid simile unique to this passage. The infinitive construct of לָחַךְ ("to lick") with the comparative כְּ creates the image of total, indiscriminate consumption. The LXX renders ὡς ἐκλείξαι ὁ μόσχος ("as a calf would lick clean"), substituting "calf" for "ox."

5. **v. 5 -- "Pethor, which is by the River" (פְּתוֹרָה אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַנָּהָר):** "The River" (הַנָּהָר) with the article refers to the Euphrates. Pethor is identified with Pitru in Assyrian records, on the Sajur River near its confluence with the Euphrates. The phrase "land of the sons of his people" (אֶרֶץ בְּנֵי־עַמּוֹ) is read in the MT; the LXX has a variant reading.

6. **v. 5 -- "they cover the eye of the land" (וַיְכַס אֶת־עֵין הָאָרֶץ):** The idiom עֵין הָאָרֶץ ("eye of the land") means "the surface/face of the land." The same expression appears in Exodus 10:5, 15 of the locust plague. The metaphor suggests that Israel obscures the visible surface of the earth.

7. **v. 6 -- "curse...bless...curse" (אָרָה...בָרַךְ...קָבַב):** Balak's speech employs two different Hebrew roots for cursing: אָרַר (v. 6a, "impose a binding curse") and קָבַב (v. 6b, "pronounce a curse/execration"). The distinction suggests different modalities of cursing. The formal equivalence preserves both as "curse" but the Hebrew nuance should be noted.

8. **v. 7 -- "fees for divination" (וּקְסָמִים בְּיָדָם):** Literally "divinations in their hand." The noun קְסָמִים (from קסם, "to practice divination") here functions metonymically for the payment brought to secure Balaam's divinatory services. The LXX renders τὰ μαντεῖα ("the divination fees").

9. **v. 8 -- YHWH / God alternation:** Balaam himself uses the name YHWH (v. 8, 13, 18, 19), while the narrator employs both "God" (אֱלֹהִים, vv. 9, 10, 12, 20) and "YHWH" (vv. 22, 28, 31, 35). This alternation is a distinctive literary feature of the Balaam narrative, possibly reflecting the tension between Balaam's knowledge of YHWH and his identity as a foreign diviner.

10. **v. 18 -- "the mouth of YHWH" (אֶת־פִּי יהוה):** The idiom פִּי יהוה ("mouth of YHWH") is significant in the Balaam narrative, where the central question is whose word/mouth will prevail. Balaam cannot go beyond YHWH's "mouth" -- the same organ through which blessings and curses are spoken.

11. **v. 22 -- "as an adversary" (לְשָׂטָן):** The noun שָׂטָן here with the preposition לְ functions as a role designation ("as an adversary/opposer"), not as a proper name. This is the angel of YHWH acting in an adversarial capacity to obstruct Balaam's journey. The LXX renders εἰς ἐνδιαβολήν ("as an obstruction/opposition").

12. **v. 23 -- "his drawn sword in his hand" (וְחַרְבּוֹ שְׁלוּפָה בְּיָדוֹ):** The participial phrase שְׁלוּפָה ("drawn, unsheathed") is a passive participle of שָׁלַף. The identical phrase recurs at v. 31 when Balaam's eyes are opened, creating an inclusio around the donkey episode.

13. **v. 28 -- "opened the mouth of the donkey" (וַיִּפְתַּח יהוה אֶת־פִּי הָאָתוֹן):** The verb פָּתַח ("to open") paired with פֶּה ("mouth") echoes the mouth/speech motif of the chapter. YHWH opens the donkey's mouth (v. 28) just as he will later open Balaam's eyes (v. 31, using גָּלָה, "to uncover"). The donkey (אָתוֹן) is feminine, and all verbal and pronominal forms consistently reflect this grammatical gender.

14. **v. 30 -- "all your life long to this day" (מֵעוֹדְךָ עַד־הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה):** Literally "from your existence until this day." The word עוֹד with the 2ms suffix means "your whole life/existence." The donkey's rhetorical question expects a negative answer, which Balaam gives: "No" (לֹא).

15. **v. 32 -- "your way is reckless before me" (כִּי יָרַט הַדֶּרֶךְ לְנֶגְדִּי):** The verb יָרַט is a hapax legomenon in the Hebrew Bible. Its meaning is debated; it likely means "to be precipitate, reckless, perverse." The LXX renders οὐκ ἀστεία ἡ ὁδός σου ἐναντίον μου ("your way is not pleasing before me"), interpreting the rare verb as describing something displeasing or improper.

16. **v. 37 -- "Did I not surely send?" (הֲלֹא שָׁלֹחַ שָׁלַחְתִּי):** Infinitive absolute construction (שָׁלֹחַ שָׁלַחְתִּי). Rendered with doubling ("Did I not surely send") per binding conventions to preserve the emphatic force of the Hebrew construction.

17. **v. 38 -- "Am I at all able?" (הֲיָכוֹל אוּכַל):** Another infinitive absolute construction (יָכוֹל אוּכַל), rendered with doubling. Balaam's point is his utter inability to speak independently of God's word.

18. **v. 40 -- "he sent portions" (וַיִּשְׁלַח):** The verb שָׁלַח here means "to send portions (of the sacrificial meal)." The LXX specifies the act of sending portions more explicitly. This is a diplomatic feast, not a sacrificial ritual per se, though it accompanies the sacrifices.

19. **v. 41 -- "Bamoth-baal" (בָּמוֹת בָּעַל):** Literally "the high places of Baal." This is a cultic site from which Balaam would have a vantage point to see the Israelite camp below. The name connects to the itinerary in 21:19-20, where "Bamoth" was mentioned as a station on the journey.

20. **v. 41 -- "the edge of the people" (קְצֵה הָעָם):** Balaam sees only the extremity (קָצֶה) of the Israelite camp, not its totality. This partial view becomes significant in the following chapter (23:13), where Balak deliberately moves Balaam to see different portions of the camp.

### Cross-References to LXX (Vaticanus)

- **v. 4:** LXX reads "calf" (μόσχος) for MT "ox" (שׁוֹר), a minor zoological divergence.
- **v. 5:** LXX renders "land of the sons of his people" differently; Vaticanus appears to read a variant for the geographic description of Pethor.
- **v. 11:** LXX adds a clarifying conjunction and adjusts the syntax of Balaam's quoted speech to Balak's messengers.
- **v. 20:** LXX and MT are in close agreement on God's conditional permission, though LXX smooths the syntax slightly.
- **v. 22:** LXX renders שָׂטָן with εἰς ἐνδιαβολήν, a circumlocution avoiding direct transliteration.
- **v. 28:** Both MT and LXX agree that YHWH/the Lord opened the donkey's mouth; the LXX uses ἤνοιξεν κύριος τὸ στόμα τῆς ὄνου.
- **v. 32:** LXX interprets the hapax יָרַט as "not pleasing" (οὐκ ἀστεία), an interpretive rendering of an opaque Hebrew verb.
- **v. 33:** LXX reverses the conditional: "perhaps if she had not turned..." (εἰ μὴ ἐξέκλινεν), closely matching the MT's syntax.
- **v. 35:** Both MT and LXX preserve the divine instruction restricting Balaam's speech to God's word alone.
- **v. 40:** LXX explicitly mentions Balak sending portions of the sacrifice to Balaam and to the rulers with him, agreeing with the MT.
- **v. 41:** LXX renders "Bamoth-baal" as a transliteration and agrees that Balaam saw a portion (μέρος τι) of the people.

### Structural Observations

Numbers 22 initiates the Balaam cycle (chapters 22-24), one of the most literarily sophisticated sections of the Torah. The chapter divides into four movements: (1) Balak's two embassies to Balaam (vv. 1-21), structured as a type-scene of escalating diplomatic pressure; (2) the donkey episode (vv. 22-35), a narrative of divine obstruction featuring the remarkable speech of the donkey; (3) Balaam's arrival and Balak's reception (vv. 36-40); and (4) the preparation for the oracle at Bamoth-baal (v. 41).

The chapter is governed by a sustained irony: the professional seer cannot see what his donkey sees. The angel is visible to the animal (vv. 23, 25, 27) but invisible to the diviner until YHWH "uncovers" his eyes (v. 31). This ironic reversal -- the beast perceives what the prophet cannot -- underscores the chapter's theological claim that prophetic sight is entirely dependent on divine gift, not human expertise.

The speech/mouth motif is central: Balaam cannot go beyond "the mouth of YHWH" (v. 18); YHWH opens the donkey's "mouth" (v. 28); and the angel restricts Balaam to speak only "the word" given to him (v. 35). The chapter thus prepares for the oracles of chapters 23-24, where Balaam's mouth becomes the vehicle for YHWH's blessing of Israel despite Balak's desire for a curse.
